coredata.dc:title: Lignin: A sustainable precursor for nanostructured carbon materials for supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Madhu, Rajesh
Periasamy, Arun Prakash
Schlee, Philipp
Hérou, Servann
Titirici, Maria-Magdalena
coredata.dc:description: After being undervalued for a long-time, the potential use of lignins have gained interest as a source of wealth from waste. Despite the complexity of lignins chemical structure, its high carbon content compared to cellulose and sugars makes lignin a very promising precursor for the development of nanocarbons for broad range applications, including supercapacitors for energy storage. High performing supercapacitors have been made using dimensionally different nanocarbon entities (zero-to three), such as carbon nanoparticles, carbon nanodots, carbon fibers, and carbon nanosheets with modular and interconnected pore structures, which are prepared from lignins of various types with and without use of templates, porogens and chemical agents. Control of physicochemical and electrochemical properties in such nanocarbons remains the key for their high energy density, flexibility, and long-term stability. To this end, nanocarbons conductivity, porosity and surface areas are controlled by various synthesis approaches while detailed adjustment of pore structure, pore width and pore distribution have been made to improve electrolyte access, ions confinement, and transportation. To compare performances of nanocarbons made from different lignin types, we must do meaningful investigations on the lignin's structure, composition, and purity to understand how their structure-property relationships are influenced by pyrolysis, carbonization and activation methods/conditions used. In this review, a clear overview of the lignins structure, classification, and properties is presented, wherein we compared the structure-property-performance relationships of nanostructured carbons from lignins for improvement of supercapacitor device performances with recent advances.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: After being undervalued for a long-time, the potential use of lignins have gained interest as a source of wealth from waste. Despite the complexity of lignins chemical structure, its high carbon content compared to cellulose and sugars makes lignin a very promising precursor for the development of nanocarbons for broad range applications, including supercapacitors for energy storage. High performing supercapacitors have been made using dimensionally different nanocarbon entities (zero-to three), such as carbon nanoparticles, carbon nanodots, carbon fibers, and carbon nanosheets with modular and interconnected pore structures, which are prepared from lignins of various types with and without use of templates, porogens and chemical agents. Control of physicochemical and electrochemical properties in such nanocarbons remains the key for their high energy density, flexibility, and long-term stability. To this end, nanocarbons conductivity, porosity and surface areas are controlled by various synthesis approaches while detailed adjustment of pore structure, pore width and pore distribution have been made to improve electrolyte access, ions confinement, and transportation. To compare performances of nanocarbons made from different lignin types, we must do meaningful investigations on the lignin's structure, composition, and purity to understand how their structure-property relationships are influenced by pyrolysis, carbonization and activation methods/conditions used. In this review, a clear overview of the lignins structure, classification, and properties is presented, wherein we compared the structure-property-performance relationships of nanostructured carbons from lignins for improvement of supercapacitor device performances with recent advances.

Separation processes of lignin from wood, steps involved in preparing dimensionally different nanocarbon entities (zero -to three), and electrolytes for the fabrication of sustainable and flexible supercapacitor devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, lithium-ion batteries have been widely used in small portable devices to large power system i.e. electric vehicles (EVs). Particularly, Li-air batteries have been used in several practical applications viz. electric vehicles, robots, plug-in hybrid electric vehicles, and electric power storage systems, comparing to that of gasoline in terms of usable energy density. However, like fossil fuels, we can foresee that the world might use all the lithium supplies in the future, which can be the new ‘oil’. For example, in an electric car (Tesla Model S) the battery pack requires 140 pounds of lithium, which can be used in 10,000 cell phones. Hence, the currently available energy storage and conversion devices are mostly based on critical metals (unsustainable), which must be very sustainable, and low cost. Moreover, the consisting materials and making process of energy devices (batteries, supercapacitors, and solar cells) were not designed based on the long-term available materials. However, recycling the materials from used devices is not efficient because of generation of toxic by-products and high energy consumption required for one or more post processing/recycling steps involved. Therefore, we need to develop sustainable approaches to make less toxic, low-cost electrode materials, where the precursors needed can be sourced from resources available worldwide. While we continue to make electrodes from sustainable materials for efficient energy storage, we want to achieve high performance (energy density and power density) and minimise the energy consumption for recycling.

Supercapacitors have emerged as clear favorites for energy storage which have been used in wearable electronic devices, mobile phones, flexible displays, garment devices, biomedical, and antimicrobial textiles due to their high capacitance, high power density, fast charging/discharging processes and long cycle life [1–4]. Among the materials of interest for supercapacitors, carbons have become one of the most suitable materials of choice to make indispensable components (electrodes) of a supercapacitor, because of their excellent electrical conductivity, resistivity to corrosion and they remain mechanically robust after long potential holds. The charge storage at as made carbon electrodes takes place via electrostatic ad-/desorption of ions from the electrolyte on the surface of two oppositely polarized electrodes forming a so-called electric double layer at each of them. As resulting double layer can be enhanced by introducing modular pores at the carbon surface as well as its interior, which maximises the surface area available for the double layer to get formed. Furthermore, doping heteroatoms to the adjacent carbon atoms introduces extrinsic defects (edges, pentagon defects) and improves the chemical and electronic properties, enhancing pseudocapacitance and the device performance, overall. Studies have suggested the external doping of heteroatoms such as N, O, B, P and S with carbons to tailor the electron-donor properties and consequently to enhance the energy storage performance [5]. Compared to other precursors (petrochemical precursors, synthetic polymers, and natural biomass), biomasses present themselves as strong contenders to make carbon structures, attributed to their natural abundance, surplus hydrogen and carbon contents, and intrinsic functionalities associated with their structure. Hence, remarkable efforts have been made to synthesize porous carbons from abundant biomasses such as coconut shells, plant leaves, nutshell, wheat straw, and lignins [6–13].

Lignin is the richest natural aromatic biopolymer in plants which can be obtained from woods, and even from the industrial waste like paper mill effluents (70 tons per year) [14]. The global lignin supply and market needs clearly show that the technical lignin demands, including lignosulfonates, kraft lignin, hydrolysis, and soda lignins are increasing, where Kraft lignin production alone has increased by 150% from 2014 to 2018 [15,16]. Despite the differently existing change in molecular structures in lignins, they all have high carbon content (60-75 wt%) and thus can provide high yield after pyrolysis, essential synthesis step of electrically conductive carbons. Depending on the plant species, lignin accounts for approximately 15–40% of the dry weight of every plant, the polysaccharides, mainly cellulose and hemicellulose, make up the rest [17]. Generally, lignin contents decrease in the following order: 25–35% in softwood, 20–25% in hardwood, and 15–25% in herbaceous plants [18,19]. Also, lignin is next to cellulose and hemicellulose, one of the three major components of the lignocellulosic biomass (living plants/plant residues) [20]. These three components are bound together in the plants’ cell walls in the so-called lignin carbohydrate complex (LCC) [19,21]. This complex protects the plants by providing structural integrity and water/solute transport which has been decisive for the evolution of land plants [22,23]. While cellulose consists of single intermonomer linkages, lignin has no structural regularity within its polymeric structure which mainly consists of carbon and oxygen [24]. Despite this structural difference, lignins have been greatly utilized in energy storage, ethanol production, asphalt, resins, and others as they favour making quality carbon structures with high yields [25–29]. Nanocarbons such as carbon spheres [30,31], carbon nanosheets [32,33], graphene sheets, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [33,34], carbon fibers, and neat and porous carbon quasi-nanosheets [32], nitrogen doped 3D porous hierarchical carbons [35], nitrogen and sulphur co-doped 3D porous carbons [36], porous carbon monoliths [37] and nitrogen doped carbon aerogels [38] have been prepared from lignins, where the porous channels and hierarchical pore structures as created improved their ion transport while the high conductivity and mechanical strengths of the carbon structural frameworks as made enhanced their high energy and power density. Recently, a few reports on lignin derived carbon materials with N, S doping is available in the literature [5,13,39]. In addition, lignin can be electrospun to get carbon fibers vs biomass coconut shell for example, and of course cellulose is quite hard to process and the yield is questionable when converted to carbon. However, still the lignin is not a well-established chemical, but naturally a loosely defined precursor, literature on structure/chemistry of the lignin precursor and the resulting properties is scarce to date. Hence, it is very important to fully characterize the lignin prior to any carbon synthesis with the most suitable techniques available to understand its physico-chemical properties and structure in order to make lignin based nanocarbons for the efficient energy storage

This review discusses the recent advances in the field of conductive nanostructured carbons prepared from lignins for fabricating supercapacitors (SCs), and wearable flexible supercapacitors (FSCs) in both symmetric and asymmetric configurations. Herein, conductive nanostructured carbons refer to dimensionally different (one to three dimensionally oriented) carbons with high porosity and surface area, synthesized from lignins which differ in their chemical structure, surface functional groups, composition, and occurrence. The most common methods that have been used to prepare nanoporous conductive carbons from lignins are summarised with an aim of understanding their structure-property relationships for achieving performance gains and stability improvements in supercapacitors. The challenges encountered during the creation of lignin derived nanocarbons and the possible ways to overcome any difficulties faced during the device integration steps are discussed. Also, special mentions herein have been made, which are the areas that need extra care to make such carbons promising for use in high performing wearable energy storage devices. We present our own views about nanostructured carbons use in supercapacitors, describing which lignin type enables preparations of high quality nanocarbons with good yields and what suitable method developments are needed to enhance structure-property, performance and stability improvements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lignin's market value and energy demands
ce:section.ce:para: Worldwide, US is leading the lignins production (100,000 t/y) (Fig. 1 A) [16]. The technical lignins, including lignosulfonates, kraft, hydrolysis and soda lignins, are widely used, though the lignosulfonate supply is significantly higher than the rest (Fig. 1B) [16]. The potential lignin market value to production/market volume (Kt/year) comparison reveals low purity lignins around 0.1 billion are utilized in the energy applications and significant money is spent towards procurement and refining of native forms of lignins. Though lignins of different types are used in various applications, kraft and organoslov lignins are mostly utilized in carbon fiber production (please refer to the 1D carbons in section 4.1.2). Broad range purity assessments are needed prior procuring a suitable lignin type to make any carbons out of it. As impurities present in lignin could enhance the performance, attention must be paid for each purification step and more caution is needed while we claim a lignin derived carbon material's performance is high or extraordinary. On the other hand, global supercapacitors market size is increasing since 2016 and the growth rate of supercapacitors had significantly increased between the years 2015 and 2020 (Fig. 1C) [40]. Different methods have been used to increase the energy density of nanocarbons, though, their surface area (20%) and pores (40%) have been proven as one of the major contributors, which means that other than the contribution seen from the electrolytes used (40% and above), both surface area and the pores introduced remain the most significant contributors (60%) (Fig. 1D). Therefore, being the two most key descriptors of energy density, efficient surface area control and pore structure engineering are needed [41]. While we expect more lignin assisted supercapacitor development with the control of surface area and porosity in coming years, what we want to see is holistic approaches to monitor their changes in nanocarbons to be made from lignins for the supercapacitors development. This includes synthesis of mechanically robust and electrically conducting porous carbon materials of different dimensions, right from zero dimensional architectures to 3D scaffolds. Also, different solubility of lignins in alkaline and acid solutions likely increases their use as a component of gel electrolytes, where attempts could be focused to have control of cross-linking, increase of mechanical strength, tuning of shape recovery, and an increase of electrochemical stability and ionic conductivity [42]. This could increase lignins utilization in supercapacitor production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Why nanostructured carbons from lignins for supercapacitors?
ce:section.ce:para: The supercapacitor field continues to expand, grabbing attention worldwide and increasing research interests. The literature shows that 1/3 of the scientific community in this area targets an increase of energy density by preparing porous carbons with high porosity and surface area [41]. According to IUPAC nomenclature, any pores smaller than 100 nm can be classified as nanopores and such carbon structures can be termed as nanoporous carbons. Also, pores of porous structured carbons can be classified into macro (>50 nm), micro (<2 nm), and meso (2–50 nm), where pore widths smaller than 2 nm and larger than 0.7 nm, are termed as supermicropores. The ultra-micropores are the ones with small or sub-micropores (<0.7 nm). According to Li et al. the term " hierarchy” is often misunderstood and mere presence of different pore sizes in carbons doesn't mean they have a hierarchical structure [43]. Lignin being valued as a sustainable precursor have been used to prepare nanocarbons mainly due to its high thermal stability and its high carbon content as a result of its aromatic structure with phenolic and aliphatic hydroxyl groups. However, proper understanding of porous structures derived from lignins is important to obtain meaningful correlations between the pores present and the ion transport involved. To this end, use of suitable high-resolution microscopy and operando techniques prior and during supercapacitor testing is recommended for the real-time monitoring of structural change/collapse occurring at the pores.

ce:section.ce:section-title: How surface area and porosity affect energy density and how the porosity in lignin carbons can be monitored?
ce:section.ce:para: An overview of literature in this area clearly points out that the most reports discuss the boosted performance and positive effects of carbon structures made from a range of biomasses, including lignins, without bringing into discussion any structure-property-performance relationships, long run performance setbacks and stability issues due to the possibility that the pore structures in them can collapse. To monitor the porosity maintenance in lignin nanocarbons in detail, exclusive investigations are needed prior and while subjecting them to carbonization and graphitization. Suitable conditions of pore structure introduction at carbon electrodes with meaningful cross checks of their pore distribution and pore structure retention are also mandatory prior conclusion of the carbon materials performance as the best, which we will discuss in detail in the following sections where possible.

Specifically, ions confined to sub-nanopores (<1 nm) and that lesser than solvated ions size can influence capacitance and energy density, therefore, the charge storage mechanisms for nanoporous carbon electrodes should be discussed based on the classical diffusion kinetic theories [44]. Such exclusive reviews necessitate the development of recent models and simulations to unravel the mechanistic pathways with use of appropriate in situ techniques and suitable characterization techniques to monitor ion confinement in the nanopores. We necessitate (i) development of recent models and simulations to unravel mechanistic pathways (ii) use of appropriate in situ techniques and suitable characterization techniques to monitor ion confinement in the nanopores and (iii) gain clear knowledge about diffusivity/migration of ions via pores for successful development of new nanocarbon materials from lignins. Holistic approaches must be developed to understand three key descriptors of performance, diffusivity of ions, migration of ions and speed of ions' migration. For instance, nanocarbons pore size can affect ion mobility, which could affect specific capacitance and power of the supercapacitors fabricated. Let us take the work by Dvoyashkin et al. as a typical example for our discussion, wherein the authors suggest five step-check principle to understand the impact of ion dynamics on electrolyte diffusion inside hierarchical porous carbons. This includes (1) determination of diffusivity via smaller (micropores) and larger (mesopores) pores, (2 and 3) determining diffusivities in carbons that mimic porosity of micro and mesopores as close as possible (4) using the proposed model to calculate the expected diffusivity/diffusion resistance for the hierarchical carbons and (5) comparing the diffusivity determined in steps 1 and 4 (Fig. 2 ). According to this principle, two results are possible; (i) the ion-solvent separation can have a negative impact on ionic diffusion in the hierarchical carbons, if the result is Dhierarch ∼ Dhierarch(expected) and (ii) if Dhierarch < Dhierarch (expected), then the ion-solvent separation can slow down ion dynamics and increase ionic concentration in micropores. Similarly, the ion diffusion within the pores of lignin nanocarbons could be closely monitored and, diffusivity estimations could be made using the pore type information derived from suitable modelling techniques such as density functional theory (DFT). The pore structures created at the nanocarbons experimentally could then be monitored using in-situ techniques such as small angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) [45]. Five-step check principle could also be used thereafter to determine the impact of ion dynamics upon charging up the transport-optimized carbon materials from lignins.

As high purity lignins are always in need for making high surface lignin carbons, we will first discuss the purity and properties of different lignins and then will discuss the most common methods used for lignin purification. We will highlight the importance of estimation of MW and surface functionality content in lignins using one or more methods as well. The methods to create hierarchical pores in carbons via controlled pyrolysis/carbonization of lignins will be discussed in the following sections. In addition to this, recommendations to determine how the material properties vary from one synthesis method to the other are provided. Despite understanding of the capacitance increase in carbon electrodes, their performance is found affected by the pore structures collapse as well. We will therefore discuss how the pore structures can be controlled and monitored to see suitable maintenance of porosity and hierarchy in lignin carbons for the best performance achievements that have been made.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge storage mechanisms in nanostructured carbons – Why pore confinement is so important?
ce:section.ce:para: Nanoporous carbon structures with different pore shapes, including cylindrical, slit and spherical types can be created based on the preparation methods used (templated, template-free, activation or no activation). Taking this into serious consideration, Vincent et al., proposed a mathematical model to understand the charge storage mechanisms in nanostructured carbons (Fig. 3 A) [47]. According to this study, proper identification of pore type and pore width is important prior proposal of charge storage mechanism. Also, pore distribution, pore arrangement, pore uniformity and interconnectivity in carbon structures need to be ensured before and after electrochemical testing. In mesopore regime (2–50 nm), the solvated counter ions can enter the cylindrical pores and subsequently approach the pore walls to form electrical double layer (Fig. 3B). Based on the theoretical heuristic model, both solvated and desolvated counter ions could line up along the pore axis of micropores (<2 nm) to form an electric wire-in cylinder capacitor. The authors studied the macropore effects in detail and found that electric double-cylinder capacitor was formed as the pore curvature was not significant. Interestingly, Grey and co-workers proposed three possible charge storage mechanisms for nanostructured carbons, which includes, (i) counter-ion adsorption, (ii) ion exchange, and (iii) co-ion desorption [48]. Such meaningful theoretical views must be considered and suitable experimental mechanistic evidences should be provided prior determination of charge storage mechanisms in lignin carbons. We suggest, meaningful analysis and identification of (i) pore type (ii) pore distribution and (iii) pore confinement in nanostructured carbons from lignins. When we aim to develop new nano carbons from lignins, we need to see how well and to what extent pore structure could be maintained and how well this can be related to the structure-porosity-performance relationships derived. Keeping in mind that the different pore structures are likely to collapse at different times of device testing, suitable cycling stability measurements, in-situ and ex-situ micro and macroscopic observations are mandatory. We will discuss this in detail in the following sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lignin's structure, composition, properties, and processing
Lignin as part of the lignocellulosic biomass
ce:section.ce:para: Understanding the structure and composition of lignin is important for the elucidation of physical and chemical properties of the materials prepared from lignin. However, exact structure and chemical composition of native lignin as found in the cell wall (called protolignin) are hardly known, to date. Nonetheless, generally accepted is that native lignins may be broadly described as three-dimensional amorphous polymers mainly composed of three oxidatively coupled 4-hydroxyphenylpropanoid units differing in their degree of methoxylation [49–51]. Depending on the botanical source, these monolignols are present in various ratios as p-hydroxyphenyl (H), guaiacyl (G) and syringyl (S) units in the final lignin polymer (Fig. 4 ) [19]. Softwoods are mainly composed of guaiacyl units and small amount of their hydroxyphenyl units gives rise to a more condensed structure (C–C linkages) with higher molecular weight, whereas hardwoods comprise a certain ratio of guaiacyl and syringyl units reducing the number of stable C–C linkages and the molecular weight [52]. Grass lignin contains all three monolignol units of G, S, and H units together with p-coumarate and ferulate units [19]. Therefore, not only the total amount of lignin found in the cell wall of plants is dependent on the botanical source, but also the lignin structure and its chemical composition.

This wide variability of lignins makes comparability and reproducibility of materials synthesized from lignin extremely challenging. Traditionally, the large-scale use of cellulose in the form of pulp (cellulose fibers) for papermaking or more recently ethanol for fuels and hemicellulose for the production of sugars have made it necessary to establish protocols to disintegrate the LCC and separate the carbohydrates from lignin [54]. The lignins that arise from these protocols are called technical lignins in order to distinguish them from the native lignins found in plants (protolignin). Since the lignins initially found in the biomass are altered considerably during separation, depending on the separation procedure various degrees of modification occur. This contributes to the inherent variability of lignins. In the following the most widely applied separation technologies used to disintegrate the LCC and separate its components will be discussed.

An area that needs clear understanding for the development of high performing materials for supercapacitor applications is lignin separation and processing. In this section, we summarize the key lignin separation and processing technologies, keeping in mind that better understanding of these technologies in addition to meaningful discussion of lignin's purity, molecular weight and functional group analysis using regular and advanced spectroscopic methods available can help with the high performing materials development. The steps involved in lignin separation and processing from wood for the fabrication of sustainable and flexible supercapacitors devices (Fig. 5 ) are described below.

The Organosolv process makes use of organic solvents (e.g. ethanol or methanol) and water mixtures together with small amounts of mineral acid as catalyst (e.g. HCl or H2SO4) to disintegrate lignocellulosic biomass [55,56]. Typically, biomass is treated for several hours at elevated temperatures (160–240 °C) under continuous stirring [55]. Depending on the feedstock and process design, the treatment time, temperature and additives can vary quite considerably [56]. The recovered insoluble cellulosic fraction is more susceptible to enzymatic hydrolysis and the solubilized Organosolv lignins are recognised as high-quality lignins [57]. They are free of sulphur, low in carbohydrate content and inorganic impurities and have a quite narrow range of molecular weights [58,59]. Due to these properties as well as the thermal mobility and low glass transition temperatures (Tg), hardwood Organosolv lignins have been regarded as promising precursor for meltspun carbon fiber production [58]. The Organosolv process has been successfully applied to hardwoods (e.g. poplar), grasses (e.g. switchgrass) and crop residues (e.g. wheat straw) [58,60–63].It has been shown that the Organosolv process does not efficiently delignify softwoods [64]. However, coupled with steam treatment the Organosolv process gives improved degree of separation of cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin in softwoods [65].

The sulphite process was the predominant technology to produce pulp (cellulose fibers) until the 1950s [66]. Subsequently, the Kraft process has replaced most of the sulphite pulp processes [66]. In the sulphite process, wood chips are treated with an aqueous solution of sulphur dioxide and a calcium, magnesium, ammonium or sodium base at elevated temperatures and pressures [66,67]. Depending on the salt, the pH of the system can vary from 1 to 7 [68]. Under these conditions, the lignin structure is highly altered and polar groups are incorporated into the lignin structure which facilitate its solubilization in the cooking liquor, whereas the cellulose fraction remains a solid [68]. The sulphonated lignins resulting from this process are water soluble and are so-called lignosulphonates. These are highly modified technical lignins which have very different properties from native lignins. The molecular weight of the generated lignosulphonates is relatively large and varies between 1000 and 50,000 g/mol [69]. The polydispersity (PD) values vary from 4.2 to 7.0. Sulphur in the form of sulphonate groups in variable amounts from 3 to 8% is incorporated [69].

Nowadays, the Kraft process is the most widespread technology for the deconstruction of the LCC in both hard- and soft-woods to produce pulps for the paper and board industry. About 130 million tons per year of Kraft pulp are produced worldwide, which is 80 to more than 90% of the produced chemical pulp depending on the country [54,66,70]. This gives rise to about 50–55 million metric tons of lignin in form of the alkaline black liquor [71]. The aqueous alkaline black liquor is composed of the residual hemicellulose, lignin and inorganics which were separated from the cellulose fibers of the wood raw materials by treating wood with NaOH(aq)/Na2S(aq) at temperatures of 155–175 °C for several hours. Through such chemical treatments, 90–95% of the lignin in the starting material (various softwoods and hardwoods) can be separated into the black liquor [72]. During the Kraft process, lignin is highly modified as it is exposed to strong nucleophiles (OH− and HS−) in the presence of carbohydrates (from hemicellulose) at high temperatures. The reactions taking place in the Kraft process can be divided into two categories: 1) degradation reactions that give rise to the liberation of lignin fragments and ultimately to their dissolution, 2) condensation reactions which increase the molecular size of lignin fragments and may result in their precipitation [73]. The key intermediate occurring during Kraft pulping is the quinone methide [73,74]. In the presence of hydrosulphide/hydroxide anions, OH groups from carbohydrates and lignin side chains, phenolic OH groups from lignin terminal units and released phenolic fragments, as well as the quinone methide can undergo different nucleophilic addition reactions. This can alter the initial lignin structure and chemistry tremendously [74].

In order to produce value-added products from lignin, it is precipitated from the alkaline black liquor by acidification (e.g. CO2 or H2SO4) [72]. The resulting Kraft lignin is filtered and washed and can be further modified (e.g. fractionated) [72,75]. Acidification protonates all types of phenolic lignin structures and carboxylate groups and minimizes the amount of remaining ash (inorganics) in the lignin [70,72]. Peculiar to the Kraft lignins is their sulphur content of approx. 2–3% which is covalently bound in the lignin structure and a quite low content of carbohydrates and salts compared to lignosulphonates.

The main issue in exploiting lignin as a macromolecular precursor is its inherently undefined structure and the heterogeneity depending on feedstock type, separation process and refining strategies. Refining process on the other hand makes lignin a reliable (homogeneous) precursor for materials synthesis. Firstly, impurities like inorganics and carbohydrates need to be eliminated to a minimum so that lignin purities close to 100% can be achieved. Other heteroatoms that are covalently incorporated in the lignin structure through the extraction process must be determined and controlled. For example, during Kraft and sulphite pulping sulphur is covalently incorporated into the lignin molecule. Secondly, the molecular weights distribution (polydispersity) should be as narrow as possible which results in a more homogeneous macromolecule than with high polydispersity (wide molecular weights distribution) [76]. Isolated lignins obtained by precipitation out of the liquid phase (e.g. black liquor) show high polydispersity indices (PDI), underpinning their heterogenous structure and chemistry caused by both the biosynthesis and the separation/isolation process. Fractionation has shown to split the heterogenous macromolecule lignin into several more homogeneous fractions [77,78]. Fractionation can either be implemented in the lignin isolation process by selective precipitation at various pH values and ultrafiltration or it can be applied as post-processing step after isolation of the lignin as solid via solvent fractionation [77]. Ultrafiltration is based on size exclusion which is achieved by applying ceramic membranes with variable cut-offs (e.g. 1, 5, and 15 kDa) to the lignin rich liquid phase (e.g. black liquor) [77]. After ultrafiltration the initial fraction contains a large number of impurities arising from the black liquor (ash and carbohydrates) [77]. Therefore, ultrafiltration needs to be coupled with subsequent isolation and purification steps, like ion exchange [77,79]. Refining as post-processing step has been performed through sequential solvent extraction [80–84]. Hereby, the precipitated lignin fraction is dissolved in organic solvents with increasing solubility parameters. In order to ensure economic viability of value chains based on lignin as macromolecule the emergence of different fractions with distinct properties needs to be considered. This gives the opportunity to establish several value chains from a single lignin via fractionation.

After lignin separation and fractionation, characterization of the lignin fraction used as a precursor is of great importance in order to ensure reproducibility and to establish precursor-product relationships. Determining the impacts that certain lignin features have on the final product is indispensable. To date, this is only scarcely performed which is due to fact that compositional and structural analysis of lignin poses a formidable challenge.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Purity – carbohydrate, ash and elemental content
ce:section.ce:para: A key parameter to be determined is the purity of the lignin precursor. An overview of the purification steps of lignin precursor that are most widely used elsewhere (Fig. 6 ). The main impurities are extractives, residual carbohydrates, and inorganics (ash). Extractives can be determined by extraction with petroleum ether in a Soxtec or Soxhlet apparatus where the extract is evaporated and measured gravimetrically [85]. To determine carbohydrates the extracted samples can be hydrolysed in an autoclave with sulphuric acid resulting in a liquid and solid (acid insoluble) fraction [86,87]. The solubilized monosaccharides are quantified using ion chromatography. The acid soluble lignin in the liquid phase is quantified by UV spectrophotometry at 205 nm or 280 nm with an absorption constant of 110 l (g cm)−1 [18,87,88]. The acid-insoluble solid lignin residue is determined gravimetrically [86]. The total content of lignin is assumed to be the sum of acid-soluble and acid-insoluble lignins. For Kraft lignins (KL) and lignosulphonates (LS), the sulphur content determined by elemental analysis must be subtracted. The ash content can be conveniently determined by combustion at 575 °C [89]. The inorganic elemental composition can be measured by inductively coupled plasma – optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES). Finally, the organic contents of the lignins can be measured using combustion elemental analysis according to the Pregl-Dumas method. The absolute content of C, H, N and S are then determined [90].

Due to the structural heterogeneity of various lignins and lignin fractions an accurate and precise determination of number or weight average molecular weight is very difficult. The number average molecular weight (Mn), weight average molecular weight (Mw) and resulting polydispersity (Mw/Mn) are widely measured by size exclusion chromatography (SEC) with a variety of ultraviolet or refractive index detectors and calibration standards (most commonly polystyrene) [87,91]. Especially for relative comparison of molecular weights of various lignins or lignin fraction SEC is the most suitable method [63,74,76]. However, this approach can yield ambiguous results as there is no calibration standard with high structural similarity to lignin [92]. Another method to measure molecular weights of lignins is multi angle light scattering (MALS) at various wavelengths: 488 nm, 633 nm, 658 nm, or 690 nm. However, fluorescence of lignin at these wavelengths can cause severe overestimation of molecular weights [92,93]. Recently, it was shown that the use of an infrared laser (785 nm) resolves this problem [92]. Based on these results correction factors for widely used standards, such as polystyrene, were determined. These correction factors can be coupled with conventional calibration-based approaches, like SEC, to obtain more realistic values for lignin molecular weights [92].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic methods for functional group analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Lignins contain numerous functional groups, such as hydroxyl (phenolic or aliphatic), methoxyl, carbonyl and carboxyl groups [19]. Depending on origin and the applied separation and refining process these functional groups occur in various amounts [79,94,95]. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) can be very useful to trace back structural changes upon lignin modification, such as e.g. oxidative thermal treatment, carbonization or solvent fractionation [96,97]. By employing more involved data analysis via multivariate statistics (chemometrics) it is possible to extract more information concerning differences within a set of various lignins [98,99]. Partial least squares regression (PLS) or principal component analysis (PCA) have been used to estimate the hydroxyl group content, syringyl/guaiacyl (S/G) ratio or molecular weights (Mw and Mn) of different technical lignins from their FT-IR spectral data [96,98,99]. To date, additional measurements (e.g. NMR, SEC etc.) are required to prove the predictive power of the established models by cross-validation. This is disadvantageous if only a small number of samples is considered. However, for high throughput and screening analytics establishing a predictive model for various lignin properties, such as molecular weight or S/G ratio, based on FT-IR data is promising [99].

Nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (NMR) is nowadays the most important and powerful tool to characterize structural features of lignin. Besides the common 1D proton (1H) or carbon (13C) NMR measurements two and three dimensional NMR experiments are also performed [100]. Among the well-established 1D NMR methods, 13C NMR is the preferred tool for identifying certain structural features in lignin. Since the chemical shift range in 13C NMR is larger than the one for protons (∼200 ppm vs. ∼10 ppm), the line widths are narrower in 13C NMR. However, significant signal overlaps cause problems especially for quantitative analysis via peak area integration [74]. Furthermore, the signal areas might be affected through decoupling protons from the 13C in the sample which enhances the signals unevenly via the nuclear Overhauser effect. A very useful and widely applied 2D NMR technique is based on correlating a carbon's chemical shift in one dimension with the proton chemical shift of its attached proton in a second dimension via pulse NMR sequencing (13C–1H 2D NMR) [100,101]. Consequently, improved signal dispersion compared to single 1D 1H or 13C spectra is achieved through 2D NMR. This technique has been of great importance in identifying new linkages and structural motifs in lignins even quantitatively [74,102–104].

31P NMR spectroscopy is a powerful method for the quantitative determination of various hydroxyl groups in lignin, such as aliphatic, and various phenolic hydroxyl groups as well as carboxyl groups [105,106]. Typically, dry lignin in pyridine and deuterated chloroform is treated with phosphitylating reagent (2-chloro-4,4,5,5-tetramethyl-1,3,2-dioxaphospholane) to form phosphitylated lignin, which together with internal standard (N-Hydroxynaphthalimide) and relaxation agent (chromium(III) acetylacetonate) is then transferred into a NMR tube for the measurement [107].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lignin derived conductive carbons for supercapacitors

ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the most common methods that have been used to prepare dimensionally different porous carbons from lignins for the construction of supercapacitors are discussed, emphasizing the role of hierarchical pores and pore structure management in facilitating the ion diffusion and charge storage (Fig. 7 ). Here, the term “dimensionally different carbons” is defined based on the growth direction and orientation of individual carbon structures made. For example, carbon spheres (with neat surface) with spherical or quasi-spherical morphology are often classified as 0D carbons in the literature as they are not oriented in any specific axis/direction in the space. When there are pores present on the surface of carbon spheres, they can be considered as porous carbon spheres. But attention must then be paid when discussing ion-transport in them as it will no longer be surface oriented and will occur via individual or different pore types present. On the other hand, electrospun carbon fibers exhibit their growth in one direction and they can be considered as 1D carbons. Having said that, it is important to clarify whether you investigated the ion transport mechanism in detail at an individual carbon fiber (small picture – close monitoring in situ) or the interconnected fibers, forming a 3D mat or a 3D scaffold (big picture – as a whole thing). Relatively, the classification for 2D carbons becomes simple and straightforward, particularly, in the case of neat carbon sheets derived from lignin as they exhibit their growth along both directions and are therefore termed as 2D carbons here. 3D carbon structures tend to orient themselves in all three directions, leading to a hierarchical scaffold with any modular pores formed in them at low or high temperature is expected to facilitate the ion transport and storage. Some 3D carbon versions have already been made from lignin, which includes hierarchical porous carbons, carbon aerogels and carbon monoliths.

Carbon spheres possess intriguing structure-induced physicochemical properties, such as modulated particle size, excellent fluidity with regular geometry and high packing density which leads to high volumetric capacitance. Compared to neat carbon spheres, preparation of porous carbon spheres is however an intricate process as it requires high cost, complicated instrumental setup (high temperature or pressure conditions) and one or more porogens/templates to create pores on their surface, which limits their scale up and commercialization of as fabricated supercapacitors. In contrast, spray drying is a well-known and efficient method to prepare functional materials and is relatively rapid, green, continuous, and less expensive. It can be operated with a single simple step and easy to optimize the morphology of generated particles by changing the spray parameters [31,108,109]. Interestingly, hollow carbon spheres were prepared from the Kraft lignin by using a simple spray drying method (Fig. 8 A). Wherein, the KOH was used as an activating agent to improve the surface area (1536–2424 m2 g−1) and introduce micro-mesoporous structures for high performance supercapacitor application. Similarly, Y. Kitamoto et al. (Fig. 8B), reported a sustainable fabrication of hollow carbon spheres from Kraft lignin by using a simple spray drying approach and NaOH activation. Considering the advantages that NaOH is less priced and less corrosive than KOH, the authors preferred NaOH over KOH. But still then they achieved a highly dispersed and highly packed porous carbon spheres with a compact hollow structure. When using such alkali salts for activation we should remember that the alkali salt can act as both template and activating agent. The particle shape (hollow structure) can be easily controlled by altering the NaOH concentration. These carbon spheres were used as highly packed thin film electrode materials for high performance supercapacitors and the achieved performance surpasses the commercial activated carbon and other biomass derived carbons. Alternatively, a hydrothermal method was also utilized to prepare the carbon spheres from lignins (Fig. 8C) [110]. Favorably, a highly ordered lignin-derived carbon nanospheres (LCNS) with tunable size and microstructures were fabricated from enzymatic hydrolysis lignin by solvent exchange method via self-assembly, stabilization treatment, and carbonization, following the synthesis method reported by Xiong et al., [111]. The LCNS are well monodispersed, uniformly sized, and spherical in shape with tiny graphitic structure in them. Moreover, the size range of the lignin nanospheres were optimized (from 256 to 416 nm) by varying initial lignin concentration (0.5 and 2 mg mL− 1). In addition, the safety test was also performed for the as-fabricated devices to maximize their practical application. The temperature, hammering and bending tests suggests that the LCNS electrodes are suitable for the fabrication of flexible and free-standing devices with excellent mechanical and physico-chemical properties, revealing their great potential for scale up. As there are only few reports available, further efforts and attention in this area are needed, where neat carbon spheres could be made from other lignin types and the most suitable lignin type to make them could then be revealed. Also, we need to fine-tune the experimental conditions such as temperature or pressure conditions and solvents (polar or non-polar) to make porous or their hollow forms with regular geometry, high packing density and uniform porosity.

One-dimensional (1D) carbon materials have attracted much attention because of their structural properties and increased packing densities which further expand the application of supercapacitors [112–114]. Among the 1D carbon structures, carbon fibers have become promising for supercapacitor fabrication due to their high electrical conductivity and flexibility, where the shape, size and their porosity were known to have significant effects on their performance. Further the carbon fiber surface can be readily oxidized through various acid treatments, which can introduce desirable functionality (C–OH, –CO, and –COOH) on rupturing, and expansion of the graphitic layers. This then can facilitate the intercalation of water and other negative ions of interest at the as generated graphitic layers, thereby making modification of any suitable electroactive components where possible [115]. Carbon fibers can be prepared from lignins by electrospinning with large surface area and modulated porosity (ultra-nanopores), wherein stabilization and carbonization processes are frequently used to fabricate flexible supercapacitors with large volumetric capacitance due to their high packing density [116].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Why lignin to produce carbon fibers?
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon fiber demand was US$ 1087 million in 2020, which is expected to increase 12% (US$ 2403 Million) by 2027. Furthermore, most reported nano- and micro-structured carbon fiber fabrics are mostly synthesized from fossil fuels (Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) and pitch) rather than renewable biomass, which hinders the overall sustainability of devices. Differing from other renewable biomass, lignin is the only aromatic biopolymer in natural plants, which is abundant and its carbon–carbon bonds are very strong. Compared with the kraft lignin, organosolv lignin is prepared by a milder extraction process, which possess high carbon and low ash content. In addition, organosolv lignin can dissolve in water, which allows the formation of aqueous spinnable solutions with low-cost polymers (e.g. polyethylene oxide) and activation agents (e.g., NaOH, KOH) for electrospinning. Their volumetric capacitance can be increased by increasing the packing density of carbon nanofiber mats by subjecting them to uniform compression. It is noteworthy that the increased packing density resulted with the increased volumetric capacitance which outperformed several other commercial and bio based supercapacitor electrodes [117].

In addition, the stabilization process/step is very important as it converts the thermoplastic lignin into a thermoset polymer and makes it suitable for the high temperature treatment, by which the fiber deformation during the pyrolysis of the lignin fibers can be avoided. As shown in Fig. 8E, as spun fiber mats from hardwood (HKL) and softwood Kraft lignins (SKL) were stabilized in air at different temperatures (190–340 °C) [118]. Following this, the air-stabilized lignin mats were carbonized and utilized then as electrode materials for free-standing supercapacitors. It was observed that depending on the side chains present in the lignins, both crosslinking and degradation reactions occurred during the stabilization process at different temperatures, which yielded free-standing mats with different porosity. Both crosslinking and degradation reactions occurred well in hardwood HKL with the most suitable pores introduced when using HKL than SKL, revealing that the HKL electrodes stabilized at 310 °C provided the best capacitive performance in free-standing configuration. Lignin-based hierarchical porous carbon nanofiber films (LCNFs) were prepared by electrospinning method [116]. Herein, in order to get the nano fibers, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O was used as an additive with PVP spinning agent. It was observed that the carbon fiber thickness got gradually reduced from 192 to 124 nm while increasing the additive in the spinning solution. Also, the specific surface area and mesoporosity improvements made significantly improved the supercapacitor performance (Fig. 8F). Based on this work and our research experience, HKL are the most suitable ones to make carbon fibers via electrospinning.

2D carbon materials have been increasingly used in electrochemical energy devices as efficient electrodes because of their high surface area, unique structure, reasonable pore structure, superior electrical conductivity and chemical stability [119–121]. Fig. 8G shows the Lignin-derived porous carbon quasi-nanosheets (PLC) with high surface area with excellent conductivity and pore size distribution which provides a very high specific capacitance of 320 F g−1 [32]. In addition, a simple freeze-casting of lignin was proposed to prepare the carbon nanosheets by controlling the concentration of lignin solution which impacts the graphitization degree [33]. The as made CNSc electrodes showed a high capacitance with good cycling stability (Fig. 8H). Graphene-like porous alkali lignin derived porous carbon (ALC) were prepared at 1700 °C [122]. The SEM images of ALC shows a well-ordered 2D lamellar structure and a bowl-like structure with uniform mesoporous structure. The ALC exhibited a graphene-like structure with high surface area and excellent supercapacitor performance. Another interesting approach has been developed to prepare both graphene nanosheets and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [34]. A facile, low cost, and KOH/NaOH activation and hydrothermal method was used for achieving an high rate with boosted specific capacitance Fig. 8I). The in-situ growth of GQDs on the graphene sheets with excellent crystalline character endowed a good interfacial compatibility during the lignin conversion which enables more electron storage and excellent diffusion behaviour. Hence, the obtained GQD/graphene electrodes possesses excellent conductivity which greatly improved the specific capacitance of 404.6 Fg1 with very fast charging time among all lignin-based supercapacitors. Though lignin can be a sustainable carbon source for graphene and GQDs, more detailed investigations need to be made and their quality must be compared with standard materials available in the market. Also, their degree of graphitization and the defects introduced at their planes must be considered seriously when interpreting their performance. Such meaningful performance comparisons can help the field progress further.

3D carbon structures are known for their hierarchical porous channels formed by interconnected pores, which yield high electrical conductivity, high accessible surface area, and improved structural integrity, mechanical strength, and stability [123,124]. Several reports on 3D carbon materials as electrodes in supercapacitors are available in literature [32,35,37,38], but still, there have been some debates on how well the pore structure (micro or meso) within their 3D structural networks can be fine-tuned and maintained during the supercapacitor testing for long periods. Also, they have pointed out that preparing ordered 3D porous carbons is challenging and pore-dependent capacitance improvements needs proper investigation.

In order to improve the ionic conductivity of the energy material, lignin eutectogels were prepared by dispersing lignin with poly (ethylene glycol) diglycidyl ether (PEGDE) in a ternary deep eutectic solvent (DES) of choline chloride (ChCl)/urea/glycerol. As shown in Fig. 8J, N-doped hierarchical 3D porous carbons (HPCs) were prepared by replacing the DES by 70% ethanol solution (v/v) and removal of eutectogel template and partial DES by freeze drying under N2 and CO2 atmosphere. Urea embedded on the template acted as nitrogen source and favored the formation of N-doped HPCs possessing high surface area with 3D framework composed of hierarchical pores, which resulted in high performance [35]. Besides, an hydrothermal method using enzymatic hydrolysis lignin was developed for 3D hierarchical porous carbons synthesis, where the synthesis parameters such as temperature, pH, reaction time and concentration were optimized for their better yields and performance gains [125]. The as-prepared 3D porous carbon possessed high specific capacitance and a high energy density of 17.9 W h kg−1, where energy density as high as 5.6 W h kg−1 was maintained even at an ultra-high power density of 50,400 W/kg, due to their large surface area (1504 m2 g−1), and interconnected hierarchical structure with rational pore size distribution. Fig. 8K shows the synthesis of lignin based hierarchical porous carbon monolith by using dual templates (P123 and silica NPs) [37]. Different sizes of silica particles were used to tune the mesopore size, though, 7 nm silica template yielded the porous carbon monolith and exhibited a superior capacitive performance due to their higher surface area and optimized porous structure. This means sub-nanometre sized silica particle templates could be the best suited for creation of mesopores in the carbons derived from lignins. Other lignins were also used in 3D carbon synthesis for supercapacitors fabrication, such as 3D carbons from industrial waste sodium lignosulfonate [126], nitrogen-doped carbon aerogels derived from sodium lignosulfonate embedded in carrageenan [38], a template-free sustainable hierarchical porous carbon (lignin type is not available) [127], nanoporous carbons from natural lignin [128], nitrogen and sulphur co-doped 3D porous carbon materials from a mixture of polyaniline and sodium lignosulfonate [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Porous carbons by direct/chemical activation of lignin
ce:section.ce:para: Jeon et al., demonstrated the direct carbonization of lignin is an effective approach for preparing carbons with hierarchical pores and high surface areas by using three different lignin precursors. In this work, three lignins which differ in their molecular weights (purified kraft lignin, organosolv extracted lignin, and sulfonated lignin) were for the first time used as precursors to generate porous carbons by direct (one-step) carbonization without any activation steps or templating agents for the fabrication of supercapacitors (Fig. 9 A) [10]. The authors found that the lignins molecular weight plays a major role in porosity control and the electrochemical performance improvement of the resulted carbonized materials. However, it is likely that this is not only the most important parameter of the precursor affecting the properties of the final materials as their electrochemical behaviors were also found dependent on their structural morphology and nature of lignin used.

Alkali salt such as KOH have been used as both salt template and activating agent for lignins to produce 3D carbons with hierarchical pores, where the interconnected pores (mesopores and micropores) introduced on the carbon walls improved the ion transport and charge storage. (Fig. 9B). While mixing Lignin-KOH solution, KOH particles were crystalized within solid lignin which act as not only an activating agent but also as template during the pyrolysis. The pyrolyzed sample was subjected to washing to remove the crystalized KOH particles to form a 3-D network consisting of -micro, -meso, -macropores. Interestingly, the obtained lignin-derived hierarchical porous carbon (LHPC) with 12 wt% of oxygen functional groups exhibited pseudocapacitance behaviour with a capacitance of 165.0 Fg-1 at 0.05 Ag-1 with good supercapacitance retention at 123.5 Fg-1 while increasing the current density to 10 Ag-1 [12]. Moreover, the preparation of LHPC is facile because complicated polymerization reactions of lignin does not occur.

One of the most abundant and sustainable industrial waste with high lignin content is sodium lignosulfonate (SL). Template-free one-step carbonization approach has been employed to prepare highly interconnected hierarchical porous carbon materials from sodium lignosulfonate without using any activating agents [129]. The as-prepared carbon material comprised of a high oxygen content of 8.11 at% and a nitrogen content of 1.76 at% exhibited moderate specific surface area without any postdoping process, which resulted in high gravimetric capacitance of 247 F g−1, volumetric capacitance of 240 F cm−3 and areal capacitance of 27.4 μF cm−2 at a current density of 0.05 A g−1. Moreover, a superior energy density of 8.4 W h L−1 (at 13.9 W L−1) and power density of 5573.1 W L−1 (at 3.5 W h L−1) were achieved with a stable cycle life over 20000 cycles. The results are highly superior compared with other lignin-based carbons.

The by-products generated during the (enzymatic hydrolysis lignin EHL-based) butanol fermentation of corn straw is highly valued as an abundant industrial waste and has been utilized as carbon precursor to prepare 3D porous carbons via a template-free method (Fig. 9C) [130]. The as-prepared 3D carbon electrodes possess high specific capacitance, superior rate performance and outstanding durability in three-electrode and two-electrode systems due to their high surface areas and high electrical conductivity in the 4.0–5.4 S cm−1 range. The fabricated symmetric supercapacitor exhibits a superior energy density of 46.8 W h kg−1 and a value of 22.9 W h kg−1 is maintained even at an ultrahigh power density of 25400 W/kg in ionic liquid electrolyte system (EMIM TFSI).

Similarly, another by-product originated during bio-refining of ethanol contains 40–70% lignin, which can be used as a carbon source. This should open up the market of porous carbon materials produced from sustainable lignin sources. Interestingly, one such by-product containing 18.9% of proteins (from cellulase and S.cerevisiae), 64.6% of lignin and 6.3% of carbohydrates was utilized for the preparation of interconnected bowl-like nanostructures with hierarchical nitrogen-doped carbon (HPNC) via hydrothermal treatment and activation (Fig. 9D) [131]. The elemental analysis reveals the presence of 50% carbon, high oxygen (34.5 wt%) and nitrogen (9.6 wt%). After carbonization, the HPNCs were composed of 91.4% carbon, 5.2% oxygen, and 3.4% nitrogen which is inconsistent with elemental analysis. The as-prepared HPNCs have a very high surface area (value) and excellent electronic conductivity (value). The fabricated supercapacitors upon tested in an ionic liquid electrolyte (EMI-BF4) achieved superior electrochemical performance with high specific surface area and energy density and ultrahigh power density (values). Recently, a first attempt has been made to modulate the pores in the carbons without using any solvent or block copolymer templating agents [132]. More favorably, a 90% of lignin loading was used for all samples with only 10 wt % of acrylonitrile–butadiene rubber (NBR) to increase the utilization of lignin as an industrial waste which is the highest lignin loading among several lignin–polymer based reports. The lignin oligomers and rubbery macromolecule were mixed followed by thermal crosslinking to form a carbon precursor with phase contrasted morphology at submicron scale. Through this simple designing approach, porous carbons with excellent tunable pore structures were prepared which exhibited a very high surface area of 2120 m2g-1 with improved supercapacitor performances. This approach eliminates the intricate conventional templating synthesis. Similarly, a template-free method was adopted to prepare lignin-derived hierarchical porous carbon (LHPC) by using a simple KOH activation (Fig. 9E). While mixing the lignin-KOH solution, the KOH particles were crystalized within the solid lignin which acted not only as an activating agent but also as template during the pyrolysis. The pyrolyzed sample was then washed and the crystalized KOH particles were removed, which formed a 3-D network consisting of -micro, -meso, -macropores. In addition, a similar KOH activation phenomenon was used with a hydrothermal method instead to achieve 3D carbon with very high surface area up to 3560 m2/g (Fig. 9F) [127].

Hard templates and soft templates have widely been used to prepare porous carbons as the porous carbons formed thereby most often mimics the shape and structure of the templates used, where uniform distribution of interconnected pores is possible. An extra etching step, where an alkali or acid use is however required to remove the template used. Hard templates provide higher stability and improved pore/space confinement effect than soft templates as a result hard templates greatly help with the strict control of the size and morphology of the porous carbons [133]. To increase the energy density of the supercapacitors, HPCs were prepared by templating method using beta and Y zeolites as hard templates without any activating agent (Fig. 10 A) [26]. Due to the presence of interconnected micro-meso pore networks the achieved capacitance (250 F g−1 at 50 mA g−1) is highly stable while increasing the current density up to 20 A g−1. Notably, more sustainable supercapacitor cells were fabricated using LHPCs, where an asymmetric design allowed to operate the capacitor at 1.4 V (while symmetric capacitor allowed up to 1.2 V) safely in an aqueous electrolyte (1 M H2SO4 solution) with an energy density of 6.3 Wh kg−1 at 1.3 kW kg−1. The obtained energy density is 50% higher than the configured symmetric capacitor using a commercial activated carbon with the maximum power ∼50 kW kg−1.

In addition to the hard templates, ice-template method was utilized to prepare a hierarchical porous carbon aerogel with high surface area of 1260 m2/g without any physical or chemical activation steps for high performance supercapacitors (410 F g−1 at 2 mVs−1) [134]. Also, it is observed that the macropore size might be reduced while increasing the solid content from 3 to 7 wt% which led to an increased viscosity, restricting the ice crystal growth due to the shear stress generated. As shown in Fig. 10B, meso-porous carbons with a dominating pore width of 9 nm were synthesized by a nano-sized MgO and Pluronic F127 templates from a pre-cross-linked lignin [135]. Here, the amount of MgO particles determines the porosity while F127 was mainly used to disperse MgO without agglomeration, while it acted as a soft templating agent. A large scale 3D interconnected hierarchical porous carbon monolith was prepared from Lignin by making use of silica templating method [136]. This carbon monolith is highly conductive and the desirable microstructure enabled to fabricate a binder or conductive additive-free thick electrode. Remarkably, the as-prepared carbon monolith electrodes exhibited an excellent areal/volumetric capacitance of 3.0 F cm−2/97.1 F cm−3, at a high mass loading of 14.4 mg cm−2 which is the highest among the reported carbon-based electrodes with high areal energy density (Fig. 10C) [137].

Furthermore, surfactant template-assisted pre-cross-linking gel strategy has also been developed to prepare mesoporous carbons by using both physical and chemical activation methods [140]. The carbons as-prepared by using this method have 1.5 to 6-fold higher porosity, high surface areas of 1148 m2/g and a pore volume of 1.0 cm3/g. The obtained CVs of the as-prepared supercapacitor electrodes showed an almost rectangular curve representing the ideal double layer capacitance behavior (Fig. 10D). Interestingly, when compared to the pristine mesoporous carbons, post activation decreased the surface-area-based capacitance and enhanced the gravimetric specific capacitance of the mesoporous carbons. Additionally, their good supercapacitor performance was confirmed from the Nyquist plot with a vertical tail in the lower frequency domain. As shown in Fig. 10E, zeolite was utilized as a template to achieve lignin-derived hierarchical porous carbon with enhanced electrochemical performance when compared with zeolite templated itch-based carbons [139].

In this section, we have compared the performance of nanoporous carbons prepared by various synthesis routes discussed above. Herein, the performance comparisons such as energy density and capacitive retentions as a function of their lignin types and their surface areas were made to reveal which lignin type would be the most suitable for supercapacitor fabrication. Having said that this comparison has only been made to present an overview of the performance data available in the literature and caution the people who work in the field that when we make nanoporous carbons from lignin we must to pay our attention to the key performance descriptors such as surface area and any experimental conditions which alter the real and electroactive surface areas must be carefully assessed. Carbons with high surface areas (900–2220 m2 g−1) and hierarchical pores were prepared from lignins, so called sodium sodium lignosulfonate (SL), enzymatic hydrolysis lignin (EHL), lignin derived byproducts (LDB), hardwood kraft lignin (HKL), lignin from black liquor (LBL), softwood lignin (SL), commercial sodium lignosulfonate (CSL), biowaste sodium lignosulfonate (BSL), and glucan biorenewable lignin (GBL) for fabricating supercapacitors. The performance evaluation plots reveal clear correlation between lignin type, surface area and porosity with that of capacitance, cycling stability, energy density and power density, overall (Fig. 11 ). Among these, lignins such as GBL and LDB enabled to create porous carbons with surface areas as high as 2000 m2 g−1 or more, meaning that hierarchical pores present in the carbon's surface have favored the charge/ion storage. The porous carbons derived from SL have surface area as close as 1900 m2 g−1 outperform those derived from other lignins with record high capacitance value of 440 F g−1. This suggests that the molecular weight of lignins as well as lignin processing and separation steps have major roles to play in regulating the porosity as well as the supercapacitor performance of the porous structured carbons. We suggest the use of correction factors coupled with conventional calibration-based approaches, like SEC, to obtain more realistic values for lignin molecular weights to have relative comparison of molecular weights of various lignins. Precise determination of molecular weights of lignin should provide deep understanding of lignin's composition, textural property, and performance relationships. As the most suitable lignin source for porous carbons is still under debate, it is clear from the Ragone plot that those derived from sulphur process (CSL and BSL) have exhibited higher energy density (34–37 Wh/kg) in alkaline than in acid (4–13 Wh/kg), outperforming those lignins derived from sulphur-free processing. The electrodes tested in acid have delivered high power density (1000–1300 W/kg), overall. Meaningful in-situ characterization studies are still needed for evaluation of acid tolerance and parallel monitoring of the carbon surface degradation as a function of capacitance contribution and retention. The electrodes of porous carbons exhibited excellent cycling performances with high capacitive retention (>95%) regardless of the lignin porous carbons. Since the curves of charge and discharge were tested at different current densities and capacitive retentions were estimated for variable cycles (3000–20000), there exists a serious need for standardizing the protocol to access the cycling stability of lignin derived carbon electrodes. It is still important to prepare porous carbons with high degree of graphitization and large graphene domains as it leads to higher conductivity and superior performance with low interfacial charge transfer resistance across the electrode-electrolyte interface. The hierarchical pores also reduce the equivalent diffusion resistance, favoring charge/ion transport.

Attention should thus be paid while selecting a particular lignin as the carbon source. Note that the carbon content as well as nitrogen and oxygen contents vary from lignin to lignin depending on the type and source of occurrence. Moreover, lignin's carbon and oxygen contents vary because of the temperature of carbonization and which in turn can vary significantly after a carbonization step due to removal of functional groups containing oxygen. We therefore suggest the use of elemental analysis and XPS measurement for estimation of the contents of doped heteroatoms including that of oxygen both before and after carbonization as well as during and after stability runs for ensuring the capacitance contribution and retention. As the most carbons derived from lignin seems to be porous regardless of the method of activation, chemical activation still seems to be the most suitable method for the creation of hierarchical pores. Remarkably, hierarchical structured carbons have delivered high aerial capacitance and energy density, outperformed commercial carbon nanotubes and activated carbons with stability and performance. Thanks to their high surface areas and hierarchical pores for making the efficient transport of electrolyte for rapid charge storage.

SL seems to be one of the most suitable precursors for the creation of carbons with high oxygen content (8.11%), though nitrogen content in the resulting carbons remain moderate (<2%). Carbons derived from SL have delivered high gravimetric capacitance, volumetric capacitance and areal capacitance with good capacitive retention and stability over 20000 cycles. However, the structure of lignin remains elusive, making the process of heteroatom doping such as nitrogen even more challenging and stringent. Among the approaches developed so far for the creation of porous carbons, template-free direct carbonization approach seems suitable for large scale production of supercapacitors, as preparing process of porous carbon is straightforward and less expensive. Hard templates like zeolites have created not only hierarchical pores in carbons but also have helped them to achieve high energy density. Silica templating seems to work well for the creation of 3D porous carbons, particularly, for large scale synthesis of carbon monoliths with 3D interconnected hierarchical pores. Therefore, selection of hard templates becomes more critical for the creation of 3D porous structured carbons deriving from lignin. Considering the cost for porous carbons with high surface areas (<2000 m2 g−1), conventional templating synthesis methods could be replaced with those methods that makes use of thermal crosslinking of lignin oligomers and rubbery macromolecules.

The main aim of the community is to develop the energy storage devices as flexible as possible while integration of fibers as spun that are inherently flexible. Such flexible carbon fibers have provided higher electrochemical performance, surpassing that of conventional SCs. While the technical requirements for fiber SCs in the two potential applications are different in terms of power, energy, volume, fatigue resistance, wearability, and stretchability, there are also many similarities such as their device configuration, the choice of materials as electrodes and electrolytes, and the challenges in integrating multiple devices. A variety of fiber electrodes (metal/metal-coated plastic wires, carbon based fibers, such as CNT fibers, carbon fibers, graphene fibers or their hybrid fibers) have been employed in the FSCs so far [141–145]. However, compared to conventional SCs, research on flexible fiber SCs are still in its infancy due to their low performance, device fabrication and so on. Some major drawbacks are as follows; similar to micro-batteries, it is challenging to produce nanoscale and pseudocapacitive fiber materials which enable large ion assembled surface area to obtain high capacitances without scarifying their power density and cycle life, notable mechanical flexibility, with additional large pseudocapacitance. By increasing the operating potential window of a supercapacitor, high energy density can be achieved for practical applications. In order to increase the operating potential window a series of individual FSCs are still required, which can be developed by asymmetric fiber FSCs with one capacitor-type electrode as power source and battery-type electrode as energy source [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of electrolytes in free-standing fibrous supercapacitors
ce:section.ce:para: Solid states electrolytes (SSE) have found promising use in flexible supercapacitors, attributable to their major roles of increasing the conductivity and working potential window, avoiding the leakage of the electrolyte, and aiming to combine the function of the separator and the electrolyte. They can function with all types of electrolytes, although aqueous ones are currently the most developed. Instead of soaking a very low-density separator into a liquid electrolyte, SSE can be made of a wet polymer gel containing solvated ions (PVA/H2SO4) [146]. This swollen gel creates an ionic buffer and most of the efforts have been devoted to decreasing the active material-ion distance [147]. Another method is to design porous membranes, with pore sizes in the range of a few micrometres to trap the liquid electrolyte inside the pores [148]. In the resulting microscale regime, the liquid can operate as a bulk liquid and at the same time remain inside the pores by capillarity [149]. Other types of architectures such as high internal phase emulsion gels (HIPE) can also be imagined [150].

Currently available gel electrolytes have low ionic conductivity, and low potential windows, and therefore, further research should be focused on exploring alternate electrolytes. Moreover, safety of the fiber based FSCs is a big concern, attributable to the leakage of toxic nanomaterials into the environment which affects the humans and the surrounding ecosystem. The toxic material leakage from a supercapacitor can be reduced by using accurate encapsulation with safe materials. On this basis, the researchers are currently fabricating the supercapacitor devices using biodegradable gel electrolytes to avoid the above-mentioned toxicity problems. Interestingly, chemically stable cellulose and electroactive lignin have been employed as electrodes and electrolytes to fabricate a sustainable FSC device for the first time [151]. The symmetric FSC device made from lignin based composite (Lig/SWCNTHNO3) hydrogel electrodes and cellulose/Li2SO4 hydrogel electrolytes shows high specific capacitance and outstanding energy density (Fig. 12 ).

Very recently, all lignin based supercapacitors were fabricated by lignin hydrogel electrolytes with electrospun lignin/polyacrylonitrile nanofiber electrodes [11]. The lignin gel electrolytes were prepared through a simple ring opening polymerization which possesses high conductivity with mechanical integrity, leading to excellent supercapacitive performances as a sustainable flexible energy storage device. Some efforts have also been made to integrate carbon fiber SCs into multifunctional or self-powered hybrid systems. Based on the aforementioned problems, it is still a great challenge to develop high-performance FSCs using carbon materials. Stimulatingly, researchers are proving that electrospun carbon fibers derived from low cost lignins is a great choice to fabricate high performance FSC devices. Free-standing and mechanically flexible CF mats were prepared by electrospinning of alkali lignin with PVA mixture as a binder-free electrode for high-performance flexible supercapacitor application [152].

Lignin was used as a precursor to prepare interconnected fibers and individual (loose) fibers by controlling suitable heating rates during stabilization [153]. Firstly, loose CFs were prepared through slower stabilization by maintaining the heating rate at 0.08 °C/min, while increasing the heating rate to 0.16 °C/min for stabilization resulted in partial softening and interconnection of the fibers at the contact points during the carbonization. The as-fabricated binderless CF flexible electrodes possesses very high power and energy densities with excellent retention which were comparable with the top reported engineered structures [154–157].

The salts of inorganic compounds were introduced into the lignin solution to modify the solution's viscosity, polymerization, and condensation reactions of lignin, which modifies overall the surface properties and porosity of the fibers during carbonization. Mg(NO3)2·6H2O was used as an additive to prepare hierarchical porous carbon nanofiber films by electrospinning of lignin with PVP as a spinning agent [158]. This process resulted in fine CFs with highly micro-mesoporous structures.
