The as-formed MgO particles over the fibers effectively created hierarchical pores, thereby helping to achieve higher supercapacitor properties compared to pristine CFs without MgO additives.

An another interesting strategy was executed by using electrospun lignin and plant protein mixture to produce high quality N-doped CFs in order to utilize them in a more sustainable point (by-products of agricultural industry) [159]. Moreover, the graphene layer structure in CFs was formed at even a lower carbonization temperature (<1000 °C) without any catalyst, attributed to the protein-lignin bonding. Notably, the CF electrode exhibited excellent capacitive performance of 410 F g−1 at 1 A/g in 6 M KOH with superior conductivity and good cyclic stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of polymer additives in electrospinning
ce:section.ce:para: Water soluble polymers could be used as additives to increase the viscosity of the lignin solution for achieving polymer entanglement. The most commonly used additives are the polymers such as polyethylene oxide (PEO) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA). PVA blended lignin mixture was used for the preparation of twisted CFs [160]. The addition of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) into the lignin-PVA mixture leads to the formation of bead-free nanofibers [161]. The obtained ternary blended fibers were carbonized in the temperature range of 550–1200 °C, where the graphitization effect has increased with increasing the temperature. The microelectrode fabricated from twisted CFs was highly conductive than the reported sustainable materials with high supercapacitor performance. Other than PVA and PEO, conducting polymers like pyrrole and polyaniline could be used as additives to increase the conductivity of the CFs. Such polymers act as a nitrogen source and help increasing the nitrogen contents in the resulting CFs after carbonization.

Recently, we reported the synthesis of eucalyptus Kraft lignin derived CFs by additive-free electrospinning method for supercapacitor application (Fig. 13 A) [162]. The free-standing microporous CFs prepared through carbonization in N2 and an activation step in CO2 showed a high specific gravimetric capacitance of 155 F g−1 at 0.1 A/g, excellent rate capability with 113 F g−1 at 250 A/g and good capacitance retention of 94% after 6000 cycles when tested in 6 M KOH electrolyte.

Consequently, these carbon fiber mats were densified to improve their volumetric energy/power density (Fig. 13 B-D) [163]. Notably, the salt helped to reduce the carbon nanofiber thickness and doubled their packing density from 0.51 to 1.03 mg cm−2 leading to 2-fold higher volumetric energy. Favorably, the ternary system of oxidative NaNO3 salt with lignin dissolved in NaOH act as a template to increase the microporosity, resulting in good gravimetric energy density. Interestingly, the areal capacitance increased from 147 mF cm−2 to 350 mF cm−2, while the volumetric energy density increased from 949 mW h cm−3 without NaNO3 to 2245 mW h cm−3 with NaNO3. Also, the gravimetric capacitance increased from 151 F g−1 to 192 F g−1 with NaNO3 by simply adjusting the process parameters (amount of oxidizing/reducing agent).

Carbon aerogels are prepared from carbon nanotubes or graphene by a complicated catalyst assisted CVD method. Among biomass-based carbon aerogels, cellulose and lignin-based aerogels are very promising for supercapacitor applications, attributable to their natural abundance, low-cost, minimum processing steps and high conductivity. In order to toughen the intrinsically brittle lignin-resorcinol-formaldehyde (LRF), carbon aerogel using bacterial cellulose (BC) was used as eco-friendly and cost-effective material (Fig. 14 A–F) [164]. The as-toughened aerogels had highly graphitized core-shell structured carbon nanofibers and LRF nanoaggregates with excellent reversibility compressive deformation (min. 20%). The unique blackberry-like nanostructure with excellent mesoporous nature helped to achieve higher areal capacitance, surpassing many highly suitable for the construction of flexible energy storage devices [165–167].

Another way to enhance the energy stored in a SC is to increase the surface area of the electrodes, which is accessible for the electrolyte's ions, and by introducing pseudo-capacitance. Therefore, state-of-the art research aims to introduce pseudo-capacitance by functionalizing the carbon framework built around the pores. In this way, an ideal free-standing flexible electrode consisting of a highly porous and functionalized carbon framework can deliver a maximum volumetric and gravimetric energy density by forming efficient electric double layer and providing pseudo-capacitance [169,170]. Hereby, the faradaic transfer reaction leads to charge transfer whose extent is dependent upon the potential between the two electrodes. This is different from the ideal Nernstian processes where a constant potential - for a metal/metal-ion equilibrium - emerges independent of the extent of reaction [170]. Even if the mechanisms of the double layer and pseudocapacitive processes are different they often occur together and are coupled [169,171], e.g. reversible surface quinonoid-type redox reactions on carbon electrodes (Fig. 15 ) [172].

In addition to heteroatom induced pseudocapacitance (oxygen functional groups in carbon materials), it can also arise from metal oxides, where the capacity of SCs could be significantly increased by employing RuO2 in composite electrodes [173,174], In search of other, cheaper metal oxide compounds, MnO2 was able to provide pseudocapacitance based on the electro-adsorption of electrolyte cations (C+ = K+, Na+ …) on the surface and proton incorporation [175]: However, MnO2 showed sluggish kinetics due to its poor electrical conductivity [176]. One way of overcoming this is to add activated carbon which can yield higher electrical conductivity of the composite and hence quicker charge transfer kinetics [177].

Subsequently, to overcome the energy-density limitation of FSCs, composites of free-standing highly graphitized electrospun carbon nanofibers (ECNF) mats decorated with the pseudocapacitive material of MnO2 nanowhiskers has been prepared through an inexpensive strategy without using any hard/soft templates, surfactants and toxic organic solvents (Fig. 14G) [168]. The unique textural properties of the ECNF/MnO2 mats cumulated with the novel properties induced by the hybridisation between the pseudocapacitive materials and the conductive CNF mats leads to new nanostructures, which facilitates the accessibility of the electrolyte ions to the electrode's surface and results in the highest gravimetric capacitance, energy and power density.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance comparisons for free-standing fibrous supercapacitors
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, we have summarised the performances of various free-standing electrodes by comparing the two key descriptors energy density and capacitive retention as a function of lignin type (Fig. 16 A and B). As the surface areas of the porous carbon electrodes are known to have significant effects on their electrochemical characteristics and their performances, we have compared surface areas of various free-standing fibrous carbon electrodes as a function of lignin type, thereby we have also attempted to reveal which lignin type could produce high surface area carbon electrodes that can be used in free standing configuration (Fig. 16C).

One effective approach is to create free-standing CF mats from different types of lignin through electrospinning to use them as electrodes for FSCs to achieve high energy density, power density and cycling stability (Fig. 16). The creation of microscale and nanoscale fiber materials have become critical for the development of high performing FSCs. The conductive CFs were derived from different lignins through electrospinning at a high voltage, followed by subjecting the resulting fiber mats to a carbonization step under an inert atmosphere. Inorganic salts such as magnesium nitrate can control porosity of the resulting CFs. Stabilization step with slow or fast heating rate seems to affect the softening and interconnection of the resulting fibers, while carbonization temperature seems to affect the graphitization. The CFs prepared through stabilization and carbonization were used to fabricate binder-less FSCs. Other than salts and polymers, plant proteins can also be used as additive to create CFs with layered graphene like structures, where protein-lignin binding seems to affect the fiber formation while the temperature of carbonization affects the graphitization. Additive-free electrospinning method still seems to be more viable and economical, provided an activation step under CO2 atmosphere is still needed for achieving high specific gravimetric capacitance and excellent rate capability in FSCs. The volumetric energy density can be increased by reducing the fiber thickness and increasing the packing density, which is possible to achieve using inorganic salts like NaNO3 that plays the role of oxidizing/reducing agent. It is worthy to introduce ionic liquids into the lignin solution for increasing the conductivity and capacitance of as-spun CFs. The surface area and porosity seem to vary from one lignin to the other. For example, lignins such as AL, ALKL, PPL, EKL and KL have been used to create CFs with surface areas higher than 1000 m2 g−1 or more to achieve capacitances such as 47, 248, 410, 155, and 192 F g−1, respectively. Of all these, CFs derived from PPL have provided the highest capacitance of 410 F g−1, attributable to hierarchical pores, intrinsic structures and the efficient charge storage. Protein-lignin ratio also found to have a profound influence on the CF morphology and performance, where the proteins facilitate graphitic structure formation by promoting the intermolecular interactions and entanglements, while the intrinsic presence of nitrogen in proteins help achieve high nitrogen doping, surface wettability, electrical conductivity [178] and high capacitance, overall. CFs derived from EKL, AL, and ALKL have provided high power density values of 52000, 6100 and 5720 W/kg (Fig. 16B). This suggests not only lignin, but its source is also key for producing quality CFs with high power density. Electrolyte also seems to play a major role in boosting the power density as in case of EKL derived CFs, where ILs were used instead of acid or alkaline electrolytes for achieving high power density. High energy density was achieved with the use of ALKL in alkaline than in acid, meaning that acid based aqueous electrolytes are of good choice for achieving high energy density. Therefore, care should be taken while selecting the lignin source and making the electrolyte choice for CF's preparation and supercapacitors fabrication. There is still some debate why CFs derived from some source of alkali lignins can deliver high energy density and power density and why others can't though electrodes were fabricated under similar conditions. Clear understanding of lignin source, structure and composition is therefore recommended to produce quality CF mats to achieve high energy density and power density. CF electrodes have shown good capacitive retention over variable cycles (1000–10000), while CFs derived from BCL seems more stable (98% capacitive retention) even after 10000 cycles (Fig. 16C). It is not clear why some ALKL lignin have retained 90% capacitance after 1000 and 5000 cycles, while some have exhibited capacitive retention as high as 138% after 5000 cycles. Further understanding of structure-performance and lignin source relationships and efficient engineering of carbon's surface for the introduction of dopants and stable surface defects could lead to the development of highly stable CFs.

Compared to common aqueous electrolytes used in FSCs, SSEs have played various major roles in increasing the stability and the performance. Chemically stable cellulose and electroactive lignin were reacted with acid treated SWCNT or salts like Li2SO4 and freeze dried to create hydrogel electrodes which were then used for fabricating symmetric FSCs [179–181,181–184]. Lignin hydrogel electrolytes prepared through a simple ring opening polymerization process [11,42,151,179] provided high conductivity and mechanical strength to the electrospun lignin/polyacrylonitrile nanofiber electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions and recommendations for future work
ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors are the promising energy storage devices which are well-known for high power density and long-term cycle life. In this review, a detailed summary of the lignin derived sustainable nanocarbon materials for supercapacitor energy devices is provided. Based on our understanding, we recommend the readers a three step-check principle at each processing stage to understand how high-quality lignin nanocarbons can be effectively made to develop high performing supercapacitors. (Fig. 17 ). As down selection and purity of lignins remains the key to produce quality nanocarbon materials, we must identify the most suitable lignin type for a particular nanostructure synthesis (such as kraft lignin for carbon fibers), and then we must perform all possible lignin purity and quality checks, where necessary as selected lignin should be purified using suitable purification methods (please refer to Fig. 7 and section 3.2.2). The lignins as purified could then be analysed for one or more surface functional groups present. After we identified the surface functional groups in lignins (such as FTIR), we should quantify them using one or more spectroscopic methods (C13 NMR, 2D NMR and 31P NMR etc.) as discussed in section 3.3.2 to get a clear idea about their reactivity in case if any cross-linking needs to be introduced with surface groups of other electroactive metal components to increase their structural stability. The surface functionalization of lignin carbons still needs to be improved to offer higher reactivity when introducing and engineering one or more anchoring sites at a time to prepare stable metal composites. It is not clear from the literature that how much lignin functionality out of the total functionality content including phenolic/aliphatic hydroxy groups is taking part in a particular functionalization linking process prior the electrode fabrication. We need to develop new advanced techniques for clear quantification of total functional groups of lignin and with this knowledge we need to ensure precise identification of reactive functional groups of lignin. The chemical modification steps needs to be carefully designed and the crosslinking mechanisms involved in the process of lignin surface crosslinking needs proper monitoring using operando microscopy to avoid steric hindrance resulting from the aliphatic and aromatic functionality in lignin [185]. With such beforehand knowledge and deep understanding, lignins should be able to characterize well with the most efficient ex-situ and in-situ spectroanalytical (Raman for structural defects and graphitization levels) as well as microscopic techniques (in situ TEM) to properly understand their physico-chemical properties and structural stability.

Moving forward, next, we should down select the nanocarbon type (0-3D) which we want to synthesize to fabricate the supercapacitor electrodes (solid-state, flexible or freestanding) to achieve high energy and power density. In this regard, please refer section 4.1.1 to 4.1.2 and other exclusive literature reviews available in the literature [179,180]. To this end, suitable synthesis method (direct carbonization or template/template free methods) which can provide high structural and mechanical stability should be sorted. For this, we need a deep knowledge and understanding about the activating agents, salt templates, soft and hard templates or porogens which we would like to use for the nanocarbon synthesis (please refer to section 4.2 and 4.3). Control of physical and chemical reactivity of the solvents, templates, activating agents and porogens with proper optimisation of the experimental conditions to be used for the steps of hydrothermal treatment, activation, air stabilization and carbonization are very important for obtaining monodispersed, highly packed porous nanocarbons from 0D to 2D. Excess use of some activating agents or salt templates can spoil the nanopore structure. For example, KOH has been widely used as both salt template and activating agent for lignins which often resulted in high surface area carbons with hierarchical pores. No doubt that KOH activation can help with interconnected pore formation, but often the pores as created could get collapsed if the optimal ratio of carbon to KOH is not used. Similarly, freeze drying techniques followed by carbonization are found useful to create aerogel and carbon monoliths. However, suitable carbonization steps need to be adapted otherwise it could lead to poor control over graphitization and one or more structural defects. The micro or mesopores present in the resulting 3D structural frameworks could collapse at poorly controlled carbonization conditions.

With these processing steps in place and quality electrochemical measurements (in situ monitoring of capacitance fading, structural defects probing at a particular point or edge defect) in practice, we foresee a significant progress to be made in the field of lignin nanocarbons. Undoubtedly, lignin-based materials with high power and energy density with long-term cycle life will become the electrodes for next generation sustainable supercapacitors and other energy storage devices due to their excellent physio-chemical and electrochemical energy properties such as high surface area, modulated porosity, and oxygen functional groups.

With the above expected field developments, lignin electrodes are likely to play a major role in the cost reduction of supercapacitors, where even more efficient and suitable methods than now to control shape, morphology, conductivity, structure and electrochemical characteristics of nanostructured carbons are expected to emerge. Having said that, now we cannot rule out the lignins pre-processing and refining costs as the native lignins regardless of the type we procure from the market have always been found with one or more inorganic and organic impurities which must be removed prior use in supercapacitor electrode fabrication to avoid any impurity induced performance improvements. This is the area in which we need a bit more focus in the future to see if we can minimise the extra refining costs. No doubt that advanced methods to engineer discrete surface functionality introduced at the carbon's surface would attribute to the development of new materials for supercapacitor application.

While we continue to make our progress in the material fronts and achieve their related developments, what we want to get more involved in is active and meaningful use of advanced in-situ techniques to monitor the structural stability of porous frameworks, 3D scaffolds and micro or mesopores present in lignin carbons and how they present themselves and respond at the real time when polarising the electrodes at a fixed potential, cycling them over a potential range and supplying a fixed current density over long periods. Such information is always considered valuable and informative for precise interpretation of the charge and ion transport mechanisms involved at the active electrochemical interface. If the experimental results to be obtained can reflect the predictions made by molecular dynamic simulation and density functional theory, we could then be in a good position to look at the structure/defects induced performance enhancements. This knowledge can provide meaningful charge storage insights at micro- and macroscopic levels. Direct laser writing method has been recently utilized to transform nanolignins (zero dimensional nanoparticles) into 2D carbons (graphene) and nano-diamonds (onion like carbons) to create specific laser-induced patterns and products [186]. Such innovative approaches with systematic and rigorous scientific activities will maximize the development of high performing lignin based micro super capacitive devices.

In commercial prospects, we need to think how good the structural stability of lignin nanocarbons be enhanced over long period device use and what surface engineering methods developed or to be developed would be the best suited to retain their structural integrity. As we see close working connections being made between the energy storage community and the artificial intelligence and robotics community, we expect a significant progress in lignin nanocarbons synthesis for supercapacitors to be made in the near future. Given the critical systematic analysis to find potential market segmentation needs of lignin with parallel development of technical, financial and market assessment strategies in proper place with suitable scientific research progress to be made in the future, the design of the next generation advanced FSCs based on lignin is expected to get accelerated further up close to commercialization.
